name: Sidhe
description: The Sidhe (pronounced 'shee'), also known as the Daoine Sidhe ('people of the mounds'), are supernatural beings in Irish and Scottish mythology, closely related to fairies or elves. They are often depicted as beautiful, ethereal beings possessing magical powers and living in an invisible world that exists alongside the human realm. The Sidhe are not inherently good or evil, but rather amoral, driven by their own desires and whims. They are known for their pride, their love of music and dancing, and their propensity for trickery. Encounters with the Sidhe can be both enchanting and dangerous, as they are easily offended and can exact terrible revenge on those who disrespect them. They are associated with the ancient burial mounds and cairns that dot the Irish and Scottish landscapes, believed to be portals to their Otherworld. They are often seen as the remnants of pre-Christian deities or spirits of nature, holding onto their power and influence in the modern world.
subtitle: The Fair Folk of Irish Mythology
appearance: The Sidhe are typically described as being tall and beautiful, with fair skin, long hair, and piercing eyes. They often wear elegant clothing made of natural materials, such as silk or linen, and adorn themselves with jewelry made of silver and precious stones. While they often appear human, they can also shapeshift into animals or other forms.
habitat: The Sidhe reside in the Otherworld, a realm that exists alongside the human world but is generally invisible to mortals. They are also associated with ancient burial mounds, forests, lakes, and other natural locations.
behavior: The Sidhe are known for their capricious and unpredictable behavior. They are easily offended by disrespect or rudeness, and are quick to take revenge on those who cross them. However, they can also be generous and helpful to those who treat them with respect and kindness. They are fond of music, dancing, and feasting, and often invite mortals to join them in their revelries.

keyMyths.title: The Changeling
keyMyths.description: One of the most common tales involving the Sidhe is that of the changeling. The Sidhe would steal away human babies, particularly healthy and beautiful ones, and replace them with a sickly or deformed Sidhe child. This was often done out of envy or spite. Protection against changelings included leaving iron objects near the baby or keeping a constant watch over the child. The story highlights the Sidhe's ambiguous relationship with humanity.
keyMyths.source: Evans-Wentz, W.Y. The Fairy-Faith in Celtic Countries.

keyMyths.title: The Fairy Queen's Ransom
keyMyths.description: Many stories tell of mortals being abducted by the Sidhe and taken to their realm. Sometimes, this was a reward for kindness or bravery, but often it was a punishment or simply a whim. The mortal might be held captive for years, or even centuries, though it would feel like only a few days to them. Returning to the human world after such an experience could be disorienting and dangerous, as time passed differently in the Otherworld.
keyMyths.source: Briggs, Katharine. An Encyclopedia of Fairies.

keyMyths.title: The Fairy Rade
keyMyths.description: The 'Fairy Rade' or 'Sluagh na marbh' describes a host of the Sidhe riding across the sky, often during storms or at Samhain (Halloween). These were seen as a dangerous omen, as they were believed to be collecting souls or wreaking havoc. It was considered wise to stay indoors and avoid contact with the Fairy Rade, lest one be swept away into the Otherworld. This myth emphasizes the power and wildness of the Sidhe.
keyMyths.source: Gregory, Lady Augusta. Visions and Beliefs in the West of Ireland.

sources.author: Evans-Wentz, W.Y.
sources.description: A classic anthropological study of fairy beliefs in Ireland, Scotland, and Wales, based on interviews with people who claimed to have had encounters with the Sidhe.
sources.text: A classic anthropological study of fairy beliefs in Ireland, Scotland, and Wales, based on interviews with people who claimed to have had encounters with the Sidhe.

sources.author: Briggs, Katharine
sources.description: A comprehensive reference work on fairies from around the world, including detailed entries on the Sidhe and their various characteristics and myths.
